content.text: Maven structures every build around a fixed sequence of named steps called the build lifecycle. Rather than scripting individual compiler calls or test runners, a developer invokes a lifecycle phase — such as

— and Maven automatically executes every phase that precedes it in the correct order, binding the appropriate plugin goals along the way. This convention-over-configuration model means any developer familiar with Maven understands the build of any Maven project without reading a single line of build script.
Maven defines three independent build lifecycles, each covering a distinct concern:
(generating project documentation). Each lifecycle is composed of a strict ordered sequence of
. A phase is a named point in the process; it does nothing by itself until a plugin binds one of its
is the smallest unit of work in Maven — a specific task performed by a plugin. For example,
is the goal that compiles Java sources. Binding that goal to the
phase means Maven runs it automatically when the lifecycle reaches that phase.

Before Maven, build systems like Ant required each project to define its own compile, test, and package targets from scratch. There was no guarantee that "build" meant the same thing across projects. Onboarding a new developer meant reading a custom
to understand the local conventions. Dependencies were jars checked into source control.
Maven's lifecycle gives the Java ecosystem a universal build vocabulary.
means the same thing on every Maven project: compile sources, compile tests, run them. CI pipelines across thousands of organizations use
as a standard gate without any project-specific configuration. This predictability dramatically reduces friction when switching between codebases.
For Spring Boot projects the lifecycle also determines when the Spring Boot Maven Plugin repackages the application into an executable fat jar: it binds its
on a Spring Boot project therefore compiles everything, runs all unit tests, and produces a runnable jar automatically — no extra commands required.
No imperative "compile then test then package" script needed. State the packaging type and Maven executes the correct sequence automatically.
is the universal CI gate — it compiles, runs unit tests, packages, and runs integration tests across every Maven project.

Any phase can have additional plugin goals bound to it without disturbing the rest of the lifecycle. Adding code coverage or static analysis is a matter of configuring the plugin to bind to an existing phase.
automatically implies compile, test, and package. Developers never accidentally skip a required preceding step.
Individual phases can be skipped via system properties (e.g.,
) without rewriting the POM, useful for local iteration cycles.
The lifecycle is fixed — it cannot be reordered.

If a project genuinely needs to compile after packaging (unusual, but it happens with code generators) the lifecycle model fights back. Workarounds through plugin execution ordering are possible but fragile.
Unlike Gradle, Maven does not track which source files changed between runs. Every
recompiles all changed files and often all files, making large multi-module builds slower than equivalent Gradle builds.
There is no built-in mechanism to run only a single failing test class without also re-running the compiler. Achieving fine-grained control requires plugin-specific flags (
Developers sometimes confuse running a phase (
). Running a goal directly bypasses all lifecycle phases and may skip required prerequisites.
Maven ships with three separate lifecycles. They are independent; running
invokes the clean lifecycle (all three phases) and then the default lifecycle up to package. They do not share phases.
— the primary lifecycle; 23 phases from validate to deploy.
— four phases: pre-site → site → post-site → site-deploy.
generates Javadoc, reports, and an HTML project website.
The full default lifecycle in order (only the most commonly used phases appear in everyday commands, but all 23 are evaluated):
— confirm the project is correct and all required information is available.
— initialise build state (set properties, create directories).
— process generated sources (annotation processors run here for many setups).
— generate resources for inclusion in the package.
— copy and filter resources into the output directory.
— post-process compiled classes (bytecode enhancement, AspectJ weaving).
— run unit tests using the project's test framework; failures abort the build.
— bundle compiled classes into a distributable format (JAR, WAR, EAR).
— set up the environment for integration tests (start containers, databases).
— run integration tests (Failsafe plugin binds here, not Surefire).
— tear down the integration test environment.
— run checks to verify the package is valid and meets quality criteria.
— install the artifact into the local
repository for use by other local projects.
— upload the final artifact to a remote repository (Nexus, Artifactory, GitHub Packages).
The diagram below shows all three Maven lifecycles and the default plugin-to-phase bindings for
packaging. Highlighted phases (darker fill) are the ones most commonly invoked directly from the command line.

attrs.alt: Maven Build Lifecycles diagram showing default lifecycle phases from validate to deploy, clean lifecycle, site lifecycle, and the default plugin-to-phase binding table for jar packaging

content.text: Think of the lifecycle as a
with 23 stations (phases). When you board at
, the train stops at every station along the way — there is no express skip. Plugins are vendors who set up their stalls at specific stations: the compiler vendor is at
, the test runner vendor is at
. You can tell a vendor to pack up early (
), but the train still visits the station; only the work at that station is skipped.
) is like calling that single vendor directly at their warehouse — bypassing the train entirely. Useful for targeted debugging, dangerous if the work depends on earlier stations having run first.
Compiles main sources only; no tests
Compile + compile tests + run Surefire tests
Build an artifact (jar/war) for manual testing
Everything up to package; produces target/*.jar
Full CI gate (recommended for CI pipelines)
Make artifact available to other local Maven projects
Release or publish a SNAPSHOT to Nexus/Artifactory
Clean build from scratch (recommended for CI)
Deletes target/ then runs full default lifecycle to verify
How to Implement — Binding Plugin Goals to Phases
Most lifecycle customisation is done by configuring plugins in the POM's
section. A plugin's goal is bound to a phase using the

<build>
  <plugins>

    <!-- Compiler plugin: Java 21 release flag -->
    <plugin>
      <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
      <artifactId>maven-compiler-plugin</artifactId>
      <version>3.13.0</version>
      <configuration>
        <!-- --release replaces source+target and prevents cross-compilation issues -->
        <release>21</release>
        <compilerArgs>
          <arg>-parameters</arg> <!-- preserve parameter names for Spring MVC -->
        </compilerArgs>
      </configuration>
    </plugin>

    <!-- Surefire 3.x: run unit tests (JUnit 5) -->
    <plugin>
      <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
      <artifactId>maven-surefire-plugin</artifactId>
      <version>3.2.5</version>
      <!-- Binds to 'test' phase by default -->
    </plugin>

    <!-- Failsafe: integration tests — separate from Surefire -->
    <plugin>
      <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
      <artifactId>maven-failsafe-plugin</artifactId>
      <version>3.2.5</version>
      <executions>
        <!-- Bind integration-test goal to integration-test phase -->
        <execution>
          <id>integration-tests</id>
          <goals>
            <goal>integration-test</goal>
            <goal>verify</goal>   <!-- fail build if ITs failed -->
          </goals>
        </execution>
      </executions>
    </plugin>

  </plugins>
</build>
Binding a Custom Goal to an Arbitrary Phase
Any plugin goal can be attached to any phase using
. The example below runs JaCoCo code coverage preparation before tests and report generation after tests, both using standard lifecycle phases:

<plugin>
  <groupId>org.jacoco</groupId>
  <artifactId>jacoco-maven-plugin</artifactId>
  <version>0.8.12</version>
  <executions>

    <!-- Set up JaCoCo agent before tests run -->
    <execution>
      <id>prepare-agent</id>
      <goals><goal>prepare-agent</goal></goals>
      <!-- Binds to 'initialize' phase by default -->
    </execution>

    <!-- Generate HTML report after tests finish -->
    <execution>
      <id>report</id>
      <phase>verify</phase>  <!-- explicit phase binding -->
      <goals><goal>report</goal></goals>
    </execution>

    <!-- Fail build if coverage drops below 80% -->
    <execution>
      <id>check</id>
      <phase>verify</phase>
      <goals><goal>check</goal></goals>
      <configuration>
        <rules>
          <rule>
            <element>BUNDLE</element>
            <limits>
              <limit>
                <counter>LINE</counter>
                <value>COVEREDRATIO</value>
                <minimum>0.80</minimum>
              </limit>
            </limits>
          </rule>
        </rules>
      </configuration>
    </execution>

  </executions>
</plugin>
Running Selected Phases and Skipping Tests

# Compile only — fastest feedback during coding
mvn compile

# Compile + unit tests
mvn test

# Skip unit tests but still compile them (Surefire does not run, but test-compile does)
mvn test -DskipTests

# Skip test compilation AND execution entirely (faster but bypasses safety net)
mvn package -Dmaven.test.skip=true

# Clean stale output then fully rebuild and install
mvn clean install

# Full CI gate: clean output, rebuild, run all tests including integration tests
mvn clean verify

# Parallel build across 4 threads (independent modules run concurrently)
mvn clean verify -T 4

# Run only a specific test class with Surefire
mvn test -Dtest=OrderServiceTest

# Run only tests matching a pattern
mvn test -Dtest="*ServiceTest,*RepositoryTest"
Invoking a Goal Directly (without triggering the lifecycle)

# Direct goal invocation: plugin:goal notation bypasses the lifecycle
# This does NOT compile the project first
mvn dependency:tree
mvn dependency:analyze
mvn help:effective-pom
mvn versions:display-dependency-updates

# These ARE safe to run directly because they don't require compiled classes:
mvn spring-boot:run          # starts the app (Spring Boot Maven Plugin)
mvn spring-boot:build-image  # builds a container image (requires Docker)

# Compare: the following IS lifecycle-bound and compiles first:
mvn surefire:test  # runs tests directly WITHOUT going through lifecycle
                   # will fail if classes are not already compiled
Spring Boot: What mvn package Produces

mvn package

# target/ will contain:
# product-service-1.0.0.jar        ← executable fat jar (Spring Boot)
# product-service-1.0.0.jar.original  ← thin jar without embedded dependencies

# Run the executable fat jar:
java -jar target/product-service-1.0.0.jar

# Inspect the fat jar structure:
jar tf target/product-service-1.0.0.jar | head -20
# BOOT-INF/classes/          ← your compiled classes
# BOOT-INF/lib/              ← all dependency jars embedded
# org/springframework/boot/loader/  ← Spring Boot class loader
Real-World Example — CI Pipeline with Surefire and Failsafe

Production CI pipelines typically separate unit tests (fast, no external services) from integration tests (slower, require databases or messaging). Maven handles this cleanly by using two different plugins bound to different lifecycle phases:
phase. It picks up any class named
phase. It picks up classes named
phase so that post-integration-test teardown always runs regardless of test outcome.

<!-- Full Surefire + Failsafe setup in the POM -->
<build>
  <plugins>

    <!-- Unit tests: src/test/**/*Test.java -->
    <plugin>
      <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
      <artifactId>maven-surefire-plugin</artifactId>
      <version>3.2.5</version>
      <configuration>
        <!-- JUnit 5 runner; needed for Surefire 2.x, automatic in 3.x -->
        <useModulePath>false</useModulePath>
      </configuration>
    </plugin>

    <!-- Integration tests: src/test/**/*IT.java -->
    <plugin>
      <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
      <artifactId>maven-failsafe-plugin</artifactId>
      <version>3.2.5</version>
      <executions>
        <execution>
          <goals>
            <goal>integration-test</goal>
            <goal>verify</goal>
          </goals>
        </execution>
      </executions>
    </plugin>

  </plugins>
</build>

# CI pipeline commands

# Step 1: compile and run unit tests only (fast, < 30s for most services)
mvn clean test

# Step 2: full integration test suite (runs against Testcontainers, may take minutes)
mvn clean verify

# In a multi-module reactor, build only changed modules (requires mvn 3.9+ with --also-make)
mvn clean verify -pl product-service --also-make
Using mvn install in CI instead of mvn verify

# ❌ Pollutes the shared local Maven cache on CI agents
mvn clean install

# ✅ verify does everything install does except writing to ~/.m2
# Use install only when child modules depend on each other locally
mvn clean verify

# ❌ Skips both compilation and execution of tests — defeats the purpose of CI
mvn clean package -Dmaven.test.skip=true

# ❌ Skips test execution but still compiles them — marginally better
mvn clean package -DskipTests

# ✅ Run all tests; if they're slow, fix them or use parallel execution
mvn clean verify -T 4

# ✅ If you must skip ITs in a time-constrained environment, only skip those:
mvn clean verify -DskipITs
Running integration tests with Surefire instead of Failsafe

# ❌ Naming an integration test *Test instead of *IT
# Surefire runs it in the test phase — before the application is packaged
# Any @SpringBootTest that starts the full context will see an unpackaged state
public class OrderServiceTest { ... }  // runs in test phase — fine for unit tests

// ❌ Integration test wrongly named as a Surefire test:
public class OrderApiTest { ... }      // this SHOULD be OrderApiIT

// ✅ Failsafe picks up *IT.java and runs in integration-test phase:
public class OrderApiIT { ... }        // starts after package, has the fat jar available
Forgetting that mvn test does not produce a jar

# ❌ Expecting a runnable jar after 'mvn test'
mvn clean test
ls target/  # only target/classes/ and target/test-classes/ — no jar!

# ✅ The package phase is required to produce the jar
mvn clean package
ls target/  # product-service-1.0.0.jar  ← runnable fat jar

java -jar target/product-service-1.0.0.jar
It runs the full quality gate (unit + integration tests + verify checks) without writing to the shared
Separate unit tests (*Test) from integration tests (*IT).
Use Surefire for unit tests and Failsafe for integration tests. This lets you run
Tools like JaCoCo, Checkstyle, and SpotBugs should run in the verify phase so that
Enable parallel execution for large monorepos.
uses one thread per CPU core. In a 20-module project with 8 cores, this can cut build time by 60% when modules have independent paths in the dependency graph.
Never use lifecycle phase order knowledge as a coupling.
Plugin goal A should not assume that plugin goal B has already run unless B is explicitly bound to an earlier phase. Implicit ordering is fragile across Maven version upgrades.
Maven's lifecycle runs phases sequentially within a module. In a large multi-module project, the bottleneck is usually the
phases. Several strategies reduce build time without sacrificing quality:
(1 thread per core). Modules without inter-dependencies build concurrently. Maven automatically serialises dependent modules.
rebuilds only the changed module and its upstream dependencies.
Maven Daemon keeps a warm JVM running in the background. Eliminates the 1–3 second JVM startup cost on every
invocation — significant on tight feedback loops.
By default Surefire forks a new JVM for each test run. Setting
parallelises test classes within a module.
— lists all goals of a plugin and which phase each goal binds to by default:
— shows which profiles are currently active, which changes which goals are bound.
in CI pipelines to pin the Maven version used to build the project.
— shows all three lifecycles and all bound goals for the current project. Double-clicking a phase runs it; right-clicking shows "Skip Tests", "Profile", and "Runner" options.
that uses a persistent daemon process and native libraries for significantly faster startup and execution.
Fixed ordered phases; plugins bind goals to phases
Directed acyclic task graph; any task order possible
Arbitrary named targets; depends= attribute for ordering
Low — phases are fixed; only goal binding is configurable
High — any task graph, custom tasks in Kotlin/Groovy
No — all sources recompiled on every run
Yes — tasks skipped when inputs unchanged
Manual — developer must implement depends= correctly
Very strong — same lifecycle on every project
Strong with java plugin; weaker for custom projects
None — every project is a unique script
Maven plugins (Mojos); bind goals to phases
Gradle plugins; custom tasks; build scripts
Ant tasks (Java or Groovy); no plugin repository

Maven resolves plugin versions if not pinned, and it may resolve a different version on different build machines. This can produce different byte-code from the same source. Always pin plugin versions explicitly in

<!-- ❌ No version — Maven resolves 'latest', non-reproducible -->
<plugin>
  <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
  <artifactId>maven-compiler-plugin</artifactId>
  <!-- version omitted -->
</plugin>

<!-- ✅ Explicit version for reproducible builds -->
<plugin>
  <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
  <artifactId>maven-compiler-plugin</artifactId>
  <version>3.13.0</version>
</plugin>

<!-- ✅ Alternatively, use the maven-enforcer-plugin to require plugin versions -->
<plugin>
  <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
  <artifactId>maven-enforcer-plugin</artifactId>
  <version>3.5.0</version>
  <executions>
    <execution>
      <goals><goal>enforce</goal></goals>
      <configuration>
        <rules>
          <requirePluginVersions>
            <message>All plugins must have explicit versions!</message>
          </requirePluginVersions>
        </rules>
      </configuration>
    </execution>
  </executions>
</plugin>
Preventing Test Phase from Being Silently Bypassed

flags are convenient locally but dangerous in CI. A misconfigured CI pipeline that permanently sets these flags would deploy un-tested artifacts. Enforce test execution in CI pipelines by not passing these flags and by monitoring the Surefire report directory:

# After mvn verify, Surefire writes XML reports here:
ls target/surefire-reports/
# TEST-com.example.OrderServiceTest.xml
# TEST-com.example.ProductRepositoryTest.xml

# Failsafe writes integration test reports here:
ls target/failsafe-reports/

# CI should archive these and fail if the directory is empty
# (an empty surefire-reports/ when tests were expected means tests were skipped)
: default (compile–deploy), clean (delete output), site (generate docs). Each is composed of a fixed ordered sequence of phases.
Running a phase runs all preceding phases first.
automatically runs validate → compile → test → package. There is no way to skip to a phase without running predecessors (other than using
which skips the work at a phase, not the phase itself).
Plugin goals are bound to phases
; a phase with no bound goals is a no-op. The binding for jar packaging (compiler → compile, surefire → test, jar plugin → package) is applied automatically by Maven.
Use Surefire for unit tests (*Test) and Failsafe for integration tests (*IT).
They bind to different phases, ensuring integration tests always run after packaging and that teardown always executes even if tests fail.
— it produces a clean build, runs all tests and quality checks, and does not pollute the shared Maven cache (unlike
Phase vs. goal invocation: running a phase triggers the lifecycle; running a goal directly does not.
— unversioned plugin declarations are non-reproducible and can produce different artifacts across build environments.
Spring Boot's repackage goal binds to the package phase
is sufficient to produce a runnable, self-contained fat jar.
